page: OVHcloud r2-60 is a memory-optimized server instance built on the KVM hypervisor. It features 4 dedicated x86_64 vCPUs powered by an Intel Core Processor based on the Haswell architecture running at 2.2 GHz. The system is equipped with 60.0 GB of RAM, providing a high memory-to-core ratio of 15.0 GB per core. Local storage is bundled with a 100 GB SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

Performance benchmarks indicate that the CPU capabilities are modest. The single-core stress-ng performance falls in the bottom 10%, while multi-core performance and Geekbench scores reside in the bottom 25%. Memory bandwidth tests show a read-only large block speed of 19.87 GB/sec and a write speed of 15.43 GB/sec, though the Passmark memory score achieves an average rating. Database operations and Redis SET benchmarks place the instance in the bottom 25% tier, reflecting its older processor architecture.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the r2-60 offers a high density of memory relative to its compute resources, making it a viable option for memory-bound workloads that do not require intense CPU processing. However, the tradeoff is lower computational throughput and memory bandwidth. This profile is suitable for caching layers, small to medium database hosting, and memory-intensive utility applications that operate within moderate CPU limits.
description: OVHcloud r2-60 is a memory-optimized server instance featuring 4 dedicated x86_64 vCPUs powered by an Intel Core Processor at 2.2 GHz, paired with 60.0 GB of RAM and a 100 GB SSD. Operating on the KVM hypervisor, this instance provides a high memory-to-core ratio of 15.0 GB per core. Benchmark results indicate modest CPU and memory bandwidth performance, with stress-ng and Geekbench scores generally landing in the bottom 25% tier, though Passmark memory scores reach average levels. This resource configuration represents a qualitative tradeoff, offering high memory capacity at the expense of raw computational speed. It is best suited for memory-intensive workloads with moderate CPU demands, such as caching servers, lightweight databases, and general-purpose memory-bound applications.
